name: Epipremnum 'Snow Leopard'
aboutText: Epipremnum 'Snow Leopard' is a striking variegated cultivar derived from tropical Epipremnum ancestry, selected for its distinctive cream to pale yellow marbling on an elongated, pinnatum-type leaf. The variegation appears as irregular patches and streaks rather than a sectoral pattern, creating a dappled, almost leopard-like effect across the deep green surface. It climbs readily with slender stems and produces aerial roots at every node, making it well suited to moss poles or bark mounts. A cultivar of genuine collector appeal that stands apart from the more common Marble Queen or Neon lineages.

morphology.leafShape: Elongated, lanceolate to ovate, slightly asymmetrical
morphology.variegation: Irregular cream to pale yellow marbling on deep green
morphology.growthHabit: Vining climber with aerial roots at nodes

careGuide.substrate: Well-draining mix: 40% perlite, 40% potting compost, 20% orchid bark.
careGuide.watering: Water when the top 3–4 cm is dry. These are generally forgiving and will tolerate slight drought better than overwatering.
careGuide.humidity: 50–70% preferred. Most varieties in this group are more adaptable to typical indoor conditions than true aroids.
careGuide.fertilising: Balanced liquid fertiliser at half strength every 2 weeks in spring and summer. Monthly in winter.
careGuide.repotting: Every 12–18 months when root-bound. These vining plants grow quickly and may need annual repotting in peak years.

commonProblems.fix: Allow to dry more between waterings; move to a brighter position

commonProblems.cause: Insufficient light causing reversion to green
commonProblems.fix: Move to a brighter position — variegated leaves need more light to maintain their pattern
Repot into fresh well-draining substrate; reduce watering frequency

items: Ensure there is at least one healthy node present — a cutting without a node cannot produce new growth
For variegated forms, check that the variegation pattern appears on the newest growth and shows no signs of reversion to green
Inspect the underside of leaves for spider mites (fine webbing or stippling) which are common on pothos-family plants
Confirm the cutting has visible roots or a healthy node before purchasing — unrooted cuttings require propagation experience to succeed

fieldNotes.title: Cultivar Selection, Upper Tier
fieldNotes.author: Aroid Aaron
fieldNotes.content: A cultivated Epipremnum selection with mottled or variegated foliage that combines the accessibility of the genus with the visual interest of a well-marked leaf. Snow Leopard references the pale, spotted pattern rather than any particular geographic origin. At upper middle tier pricing for a cultivar selection that occupies a niche between the commodity Epipremnum and the more demanding collector genera. Whether you find the cultivar name charming or slightly excessive is a matter of temperament.
